pattern: The count of even numbers up to any value V that have been removed is just a prefix-sum lookup over the strictly increasing array, so binary searching on V for the point where 'evens up to V minus removed evens up to V' first reaches k finds the k-th remaining even directly.
hints: For any candidate value V, the number of even integers from 2 up to V is simply floor(V / 2); subtracting how many of the removed values (the ones inside the queried subarray) are at most V tells you how many remain up to V.

Because nums is strictly increasing, the elements of a subarray that are at most V form a prefix of that subarray, so 'how many removed evens are <= V' is a single lookup once you have a prefix count of even values and can locate V's position with a binary search over the whole array.

Binary search directly on the candidate value V for the smallest one where (evens up to V) minus (removed evens up to V, restricted to the query's subarray) is at least k -- that V is always exactly the k-th remaining even number.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, three queries over the same array
statement example, a different array and query set
a single-element array with the only possible query
the subarray removes several consecutive evens, so k must skip past all of them
an all-odd subarray removes nothing, so the answer is simply 2 * k
the query only covers part of the array, so evens outside the queried subarray must NOT be treated as removed
l equals r, a single-element subarray that happens to be odd, so nothing is removed

stress.description: random strictly increasing arrays with random subarray queries and a wide spread of k values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = []
    cur = 0
    for _ in range(n):
        cur += rng.randint(1, 5)
        nums.append(cur)
    q = max(1, min(n, 50))
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q):
        l = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        r = rng.randint(l, n - 1)
        k = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
        queries.append([l, r, k])
    return [nums, queries]
